title: Market equilibrium and the price mechanism
summary: Demand and supply come together here. This lesson shows how a competitive market settles at equilibrium, why disequilibrium (excess demand or excess supply) cannot last, and how the price mechanism performs its signalling, incentive and rationing functions. It then works through how shifts in demand and supply move the equilibrium, introduces consumer and producer surplus, and explains why the equilibrium point is allocatively efficient.
learningObjectives: Explain market equilibrium and identify the equilibrium price and quantity on a diagram.
Analyse disequilibrium — excess demand (shortage) and excess supply (surplus) — and explain the price adjustment that restores equilibrium.
Explain the signalling, incentive and rationing functions of the price mechanism in allocating scarce resources.
Analyse how shifts in demand and supply change the equilibrium price and quantity.
Define consumer surplus and producer surplus and explain why the competitive equilibrium is allocatively efficient.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Market's Balancing Point
simpleExplanation.summary: Market equilibrium is the price at which the quantity buyers want to buy exactly equals the quantity sellers want to sell. If the price is anywhere else, a shortage or a surplus appears and pushes the price back towards balance — no one has to organise this; the price does it automatically.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a fish market at the end of the day. Set the price too high and the stall is left with unsold fish going off in the sun (a surplus) — so the seller shouts a lower price. Set it too low and a crowd forms and the fish vanish in minutes, leaving buyers empty-handed (a shortage) — so the seller nudges the price up. The price keeps moving until exactly the day's catch is sold to exactly the people willing to pay for it. That self-correcting price is the market's balancing point.
steps: Draw demand (downward) and supply (upward) on a Price–Quantity graph; where they cross is equilibrium (P*, Q*).
Above P*, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — a surplus — so price falls.
Below P*, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied — a shortage — so price rises.
A shift in demand or supply moves the intersection, giving a new equilibrium price and quantity.

flashcards.back: The state where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, so there is no tendency for price to change. On a diagram it is where the demand and supply curves intersect.
The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. Also called the market-clearing price because every unit offered is bought.
The quantity bought and sold when the market is in equilibrium, at price P*.
Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, occurring when price is ABOVE equilibrium. Unsold stock forces sellers to cut price, moving the market down towards P*.
Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, occurring when price is BELOW equilibrium. Competing buyers bid the price up, moving the market up towards P*.
The way changing prices in a free market coordinate the decisions of independent buyers and sellers, allocating scarce resources without any central planner — Adam Smith's 'invisible hand'.
Prices carry information. A rising price signals that a good is relatively scarce or wanted, telling producers to make more and consumers to economise; a falling price signals the reverse.

Prices motivate action. Higher prices (and the profit they promise) give producers an incentive to supply more and shift resources into that market; lower prices give consumers an incentive to buy more.
When a good is scarce its price rises, rationing the limited quantity to those willing and able to pay — this answers the 'for whom' question of resource allocation.
The difference between the maximum price consumers are willing to pay and the price they actually pay. On a diagram it is the area BELOW the demand curve and ABOVE the price, up to Q*.
The difference between the price producers actually receive and the minimum they would have accepted. On a diagram it is the area ABOVE the supply curve and BELOW the price, up to Q*.

Resources are allocated so that social (community) surplus — consumer surplus plus producer surplus — is maximised. In a competitive market this occurs at equilibrium, where the demand price equals the supply price (marginal benefit = marginal cost).

A change in the good's OWN price causes a movement ALONG a curve (change in quantity demanded/supplied). A change in any OTHER determinant SHIFTS the whole curve (change in demand/supply). Confusing these is heavily penalised.

quickCheck.prompt: At a price ABOVE the equilibrium price, a competitive market will experience:
options: Excess demand, and price will rise
Excess supply, and price will fall
Equilibrium, with no pressure on price
Excess demand, and price will fall
quickCheck.answer: Excess supply, and price will fall

quickCheck.prompt: A shortage in a free market is eliminated because:
options: The government sets a maximum price
Rising price reduces quantity demanded and raises quantity supplied
The demand curve shifts left
The supply curve shifts left
quickCheck.answer: Rising price reduces quantity demanded and raises quantity supplied

quickCheck.prompt: When a rising price tells producers to devote more resources to a good, the price mechanism is performing its:
options: Rationing function
Signalling function
Redistribution function
Regulatory function

quickCheck.prompt: An increase in demand (a rightward shift), supply unchanged, causes the new equilibrium to have:
options: Lower price and lower quantity
Higher price and lower quantity
Higher price and higher quantity
Lower price and higher quantity

quickCheck.prompt: Consumer surplus is best described as the area:
options: Above the supply curve and below the price
Below the demand curve and above the price
Below both curves up to equilibrium
Between the two curves at a price above equilibrium
quickCheck.answer: Below the demand curve and above the price

faq.q: Is the market always in equilibrium?
faq.a: In theory a free market tends towards equilibrium, but the determinants of demand and supply change constantly, so the equilibrium point is a moving target and real markets are usually adjusting towards it rather than sitting exactly on it. Government interventions such as price ceilings and price floors can also deliberately hold a market away from equilibrium.

faq.q: What is the difference between a change in 'quantity demanded' and a change in 'demand'?
faq.a: A change in 'quantity demanded' is caused ONLY by a change in the good's own price and is a movement along the existing demand curve. A change in 'demand' is caused by a non-price determinant (income, tastes, the price of a related good, etc.) and shifts the whole curve left or right. The same distinction applies to supply.

faq.q: How can I tell whether price or quantity is 'indeterminate' when both curves shift?
faq.a: If demand and supply both shift, one of price or quantity has a certain direction and the other depends on the relative SIZE of the shifts. For example, if demand rises and supply rises, quantity definitely increases, but price could rise, fall or stay the same depending on which shift is larger. Say what is certain, then explain that the other outcome is ambiguous.

sections.content: Why does a cup of coffee cost what it does, and why does a new phone get cheaper a few months after launch? The answer is the meeting of demand and supply. In this lesson we bring the two curves together to find market equilibrium, watch what happens when the market is knocked off balance, and see how the price mechanism quietly allocates scarce resources across an entire economy without anyone being in charge.
What market equilibrium means

In any market, buyers and sellers pull in opposite directions: buyers prefer low prices, sellers prefer high ones. Market equilibrium is the compromise between them. It is the price at which the quantity consumers are willing and able to buy exactly equals the quantity producers are willing and able to sell. The market is then said to 'clear' — every unit offered for sale is bought, and every buyer willing to pay the going price finds a seller. With both sides satisfied, there is no internal pressure for price to move; it will only change if an outside factor shifts demand or supply.

items: Equilibrium is where the demand and supply curves intersect.
At equilibrium, quantity demanded ($Q_d$) = quantity supplied ($Q_s$).
The price there is the equilibrium (market-clearing) price, $P^*$.
The quantity there is the equilibrium quantity, $Q^*$.

sections.content: Disequilibrium: how price does the correcting

When the ruling price differs from $P^*$ the market is in disequilibrium, showing up as either excess supply or excess demand. The key insight is that neither can persist in a free market — the price itself moves to remove the imbalance.

**Excess supply (a surplus)** occurs when price is set **above** $P^*$. The high price encourages producers to offer a lot but discourages buyers, so $Q_s > Q_d$. Left with unsold stock, sellers cut their prices to shift it. As price falls, quantity supplied contracts (a movement down along the supply curve) and quantity demanded expands (a movement down along the demand curve). The gap narrows until, at $P^*$, it closes entirely.

**Excess demand (a shortage)** occurs when price is set **below** $P^*$. The low price attracts many buyers but makes supplying unprofitable, so $Q_d > Q_s$. With too many buyers chasing too few goods, sellers realise they can charge more and buyers bid against one another. As price rises, quantity demanded contracts and quantity supplied expands — both movements along their respective curves — until the shortage is eliminated at $P^*$. Notice that the curves themselves do NOT move here; only the price adjusts along fixed curves.

items: Price above $P^*$ → excess supply → price falls back to $P^*$.
Price below $P^*$ → excess demand → price rises back to $P^*$.
The size of the surplus or shortage is the horizontal gap between the curves at the ruling price ($|Q_s - Q_d|$).
Disequilibrium is corrected by movements ALONG the curves, not by shifts of them.

sections.question: Worked example 1 — Correcting a shortage. Tickets for a popular concert are on sale at £40, which is below the equilibrium price. Using a fully labelled demand and supply diagram, explain the situation in the market and the process by which it returns to equilibrium.
sections.solution:
**1. Diagram.** On axes labelled 'Price (£)' and 'Quantity of tickets', draw a downward-sloping demand curve D and an upward-sloping supply curve S crossing at equilibrium ($P^*, Q^*$). Mark £40 on the price axis BELOW $P^*$. Read horizontally from £40: to the supply curve gives $Q_s$, to the demand curve gives $Q_d$, with $Q_d > Q_s$. The horizontal distance $Q_d - Q_s$ is the shortage.

**2. Identify the disequilibrium.** At £40 the quantity of tickets demanded exceeds the quantity supplied, so there is excess demand (a shortage) equal to $Q_d - Q_s$. Many fans willing to buy at £40 cannot get a ticket.

**3. Price adjustment.** The shortage creates upward pressure on price. Buyers compete for the scarce tickets — some offer more, and resellers charge higher prices — while sellers see they can raise the price without losing customers. As the price rises from £40 towards $P^*$:
- quantity demanded falls (a movement up along D) as fewer fans are willing to pay the higher price;
- quantity supplied rises (a movement up along S) as the higher price makes it worthwhile to release more seats.

**4. New position.** The two adjustments continue until price reaches $P^*$, where $Q_d = Q_s$ and the shortage is gone. The market has cleared — through price movement alone, with no shift of either curve.

sections.content: Shifts in demand and supply: finding the new equilibrium

Equilibrium only changes when a non-price determinant shifts a curve. A useful three-step routine handles every case: (1) state the starting equilibrium; (2) identify the determinant that changed, which curve shifts and in which direction; (3) read off the new equilibrium and compare price and quantity. Because buyers and sellers are interdependent, a change originating on one side of the market always works its way through to the other via the price.

items: **Demand rises (shifts right):** price ↑, quantity ↑.
**Demand falls (shifts left):** price ↓, quantity ↓.
**Supply rises (shifts right):** price ↓, quantity ↑.
**Supply falls (shifts left):** price ↑, quantity ↓.
**Both curves shift:** one of price or quantity is certain; the other depends on the RELATIVE size of the two shifts and may be indeterminate.

sections.question: Worked example 2 — A shift and the new equilibrium. The market for coffee is initially in equilibrium. A cold snap destroys part of the coffee-bean harvest, raising producers' costs. Using a diagram, explain the effect on the equilibrium price and quantity of coffee. Then confirm the direction with the linear functions $Q_d = 300 - 20P$ and, after the shock, $Q_s = -60 + 20P$ (P in £, Q in thousands of cups per day), given that the original supply was $Q_s = 40 + 20P$.
sections.solution:
**Diagram and reasoning.**
1. **Initial equilibrium:** demand D and supply $S_1$ cross at ($P_1, Q_1$).
2. **The shock:** a worse harvest and higher input costs are a supply determinant, not a price change, so the SUPPLY curve shifts LEFT to $S_2$ (less is supplied at every price). Demand is unaffected and does not move.
3. **New equilibrium:** $S_2$ cuts D at a higher price and lower quantity, ($P_2, Q_2$). So the equilibrium price of coffee RISES and the equilibrium quantity FALLS. Intuitively, at the old price $P_1$ the leftward shift creates a shortage, and the price is bid up until the market clears again.

**Numerical confirmation.**
*Original equilibrium:* set $Q_d = Q_{s1}$: $300 - 20P = 40 + 20P \Rightarrow 260 = 40P \Rightarrow P_1 = 6.5$, and $Q_1 = 300 - 20(6.5) = 170$ (thousand cups).
*After the leftward supply shift:* set $Q_d = Q_{s2}$: $300 - 20P = -60 + 20P \Rightarrow 360 = 40P \Rightarrow P_2 = 9$, and $Q_2 = 300 - 20(9) = 120$ (thousand cups).

**Result:** price rises from £6.50 to £9 and quantity falls from 170 to 120 thousand cups per day — exactly the direction the diagram predicts for a leftward supply shift (price ↑, quantity ↓).

sections.content: For any 'what happens to the market' question, run the three steps out loud in your answer: (1) initial equilibrium, (2) which determinant changed and which curve shifts (and why), (3) the new equilibrium with the direction of price AND quantity. Always anchor it to a large, fully labelled diagram — and make sure you SHIFT a curve rather than sliding a point, since a change in a non-price determinant never causes a movement along the same curve.
The price mechanism: signalling, incentive and rationing

No official decides what an economy should produce, how, and for whom. In a market economy those decisions emerge from the price mechanism — Adam Smith's 'invisible hand'. Through constantly changing prices, the independent choices of millions of buyers and sellers are coordinated. It is worth seeing the three functions as one continuous story rather than three separate ideas: a scarcity shows up as a rising price, which simultaneously signals, incentivises and rations.

items: **Signalling:** prices transmit information. A rising price signals that a good is relatively scarce or increasingly wanted, telling producers to expand output and consumers to economise; a falling price signals the opposite.

**Incentive:** prices motivate. Higher prices and the profit they promise give producers a reason to supply more and to move resources INTO that market; lower prices give consumers a reason to buy more.
**Rationing:** prices distribute scarce goods. When demand outstrips supply the price rises, allocating the limited quantity to those willing and able to pay — this settles the 'for whom' question.

sections.content: Example in action: a bad harvest makes wheat scarce. The rising wheat price SIGNALS the scarcity, gives farmers an INCENTIVE to plant more next season and buyers an incentive to switch to substitutes, and RATIONS this year's smaller crop to the buyers who value it most. Resources are reallocated — all without a central planner.
Consumer and producer surplus, and why equilibrium is efficient

The demand curve shows the maximum price buyers are willing to pay for each unit; the supply curve shows the minimum price sellers would accept. Because everyone trades at the single equilibrium price, most buyers and sellers do better than their limit — and the two surpluses measure that gain.

items: **Consumer surplus** is the area BELOW the demand curve and ABOVE the price, up to $Q^*$ — the benefit buyers get from paying less than their maximum willingness to pay.
**Producer surplus** is the area ABOVE the supply curve and BELOW the price, up to $Q^*$ — the benefit sellers get from receiving more than their minimum acceptable price.
**Social (community) surplus** = consumer surplus + producer surplus, the total welfare the market generates.

sections.content: At the competitive equilibrium, social surplus is as large as it can be — this is **allocative efficiency**. Here the price on the demand curve (society's marginal benefit from the last unit) equals the price on the supply curve (the marginal cost of producing it), so the 'right' amount is produced: neither too little nor too much. Produce below $Q^*$ and there are willing buyers whose benefit exceeds the cost of supplying them, so a mutually beneficial trade is being missed. Produce above $Q^*$ and the cost of the extra units exceeds the benefit. Only at $Q^*$, where marginal benefit = marginal cost, is community surplus maximised and resources allocated efficiently — the deeper reason economists care about equilibrium.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing a shift with a movement** — a change in the good's own price is a movement ALONG a curve; a change in any other determinant SHIFTS the curve. Sliding a point when you should shift a curve (or vice versa) loses marks quickly.
**Shifting the wrong curve — or both — in a disequilibrium question** — correcting a simple shortage or surplus involves price moving ALONG fixed curves, not the curves shifting.
**Vague 'price goes up' claims** — always state the direction of BOTH price and quantity, and explain the excess demand/supply that drives the adjustment.
**Unlabelled or unexplained diagrams** — a diagram earns full marks only when axes, curves, original and new equilibria are labelled AND referred to in the written explanation.
**Mislabelling surplus areas** — consumer surplus is below demand and above price; producer surplus is above supply and below price. Swapping them is a common slip.
**Claiming an outcome is certain when both curves shift** — when demand and supply both move, only one of price or quantity is determinate; say which, and flag the other as ambiguous.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using a demand and supply diagram, how the price mechanism eliminates excess demand in a market. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Excess demand (a shortage) exists when the market price is BELOW the equilibrium price, so the quantity demanded exceeds the quantity supplied. The price mechanism removes it automatically through the signalling, incentive and rationing effects of a rising price.

*[Diagram: axes 'Price' (vertical) and 'Quantity' (horizontal). A downward-sloping demand curve D and an upward-sloping supply curve S intersect at equilibrium (P*, Q*). A price $P_1$ is marked BELOW P*. Reading across from $P_1$: quantity demanded $Q_d$ (on D) lies to the RIGHT of quantity supplied $Q_s$ (on S). The horizontal gap $Q_d - Q_s$ is labelled 'excess demand'. Arrows on the price axis show price rising from $P_1$ up to P*.]*

At the initial price $P_1$, quantity demanded ($Q_d$) is greater than quantity supplied ($Q_s$); the horizontal distance between them is the excess demand shown on the diagram. Because many buyers are chasing too few goods, the shortage SIGNALS that the good is scarce and creates upward pressure on the price. As the price rises from $P_1$ towards P*, two things happen simultaneously along the fixed curves: the higher price gives producers an INCENTIVE to supply more, so quantity supplied expands (a movement up along S), while it discourages some buyers, so quantity demanded contracts (a movement up along D). The rising price also RATIONS the good to those willing and able to pay. These movements continue until, at the equilibrium price P*, quantity demanded equals quantity supplied (Q*), the excess demand is eliminated and the market clears. No curve has shifted — the price alone has done the work.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is FULLY explained, relevant terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT, and a relevant diagram is included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** relevant economic theory is explained, relevant terms are mostly used appropriately, and a relevant diagram is included and explained.
- **5–6:** the question is partially addressed, relevant economic theory is PARTLY explained, some relevant terms are used, and a relevant diagram is included.
- **3–4:** relevant economic theory is DESCRIBED, and some relevant terms are used.
- **1–2:** the response is not relevant to the question.

Why this answer reaches the top band (9–10):
- ✅ Addresses the exact demand — it explains the ELIMINATION of excess demand, not just what a shortage is.
- ✅ Theory fully explained: the shortage, the rising price, the movements along both curves and the return to P* are set out as a causal chain, not merely named.
- ✅ Terms used appropriately throughout: excess demand, quantity demanded/supplied, equilibrium, signalling, incentive, rationing, movement along the curve.
- ✅ Diagram included AND fully explained: the axes, curves, $P_1$, P*, $Q_d$, $Q_s$ and the excess-demand gap are all labelled and then referenced in the words. An answer that merely DESCRIBED a shortage with an unexplained diagram would sit in the 3–4 or 5–6 band — explaining the diagram rather than just drawing it is what lifts it to the top.

sections.content: That is exactly what the Practice button below does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband, telling you which band you reached and why. The single habit that moves you up the bands: EXPLAIN your diagram — reference every labelled point in your written argument — rather than just drawing it.
Where this leads

Equilibrium and the price mechanism are the benchmark for everything that follows. When later topics ask what a price ceiling, a price floor, a tax or a subsidy 'does', the question is always the same: how does it push the market away from the efficient equilibrium, and what happens to consumer and producer surplus as a result? Master the balancing act here and market failure and government intervention become variations on one familiar diagram.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: explain how the price mechanism eliminates excess demand
